data.title: Consider Shares Of Contango Ore: Poised For Higher Profitability Even With Worse-Than-Expected AISC
data.description:
A Hold Recommendation For Shares Of Contango Ore, Inc.

This analysis suggests a hold recommendation on NYSE-listed shares of Contango Ore, Inc. (NYSE:CTGO), an Alaska-based company engaged in gold exploration and production at Manh Choh, a high-grade gold project on lands of the Alaska Native Village of Tetlin. Contango has an interest in this project through a joint venture with Kinross Gold Corporation (KGC) (K:CA).

The share price has fallen sharply recently due to the company's disappointing estimates on its AISC/oz, but shares could fall even further, as we will explain later. Nevertheless, this stock is well positioned to rebound significantly as the growth prospects for gold prices are bright and Manh Choh has finally started producing the yellow metal in the third quarter of 2024. However, we advise waiting for the formation of even lower share prices than currently.

Let's start with the gold price.

The Gold Price Outlook

The price of gold has risen by almost 44% over the past 12 months, as evidenced by the increase in the troy ounce trading via the Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR). At the time of writing, the security was valued by the market at $2,917.26/troy ounce.

The price of gold is being driven by the Federal Reserve's one percent interest rate cut in 2024, as well as expectations that the US central bank will continue to cut rates later in the year. Lower interest rates increase the attractiveness of the yellow metal in competition with US Treasuries.

The yellow metal is also used as a safe haven as investors use it to protect the value of their investments from headwinds. Headwinds are plentiful at the moment, triggered by the geopolitical risk fueled by the conflict in Ukraine, the crisis in the Middle East and other tensions between countries around the world. Tensions are further exacerbated by the tariff war between the US and other North American countries, such as Canada and Mexico, and the US also engages in a tariff war against China and threats to increase tariffs on imports from the EU as well.

Due to the factors that have a positive impact on demand and sentiment towards gold, relevant experts predict even higher prices per ounce. As mentioned earlier, gold has broken several records in its endless rise, but based on analysts' gold price forecasts, the saturation point is far from being felt in the market.

According to Seeking Alpha:

UBS lifted its gold price target to $3,000/oz over the next 12 months, […]“The global gold rush continues, with the yellow metal again outshining other asset classes year-to-date as escalating trade tensions and rising geopolitical uncertainties draw investors toward gold as a portfolio diversifier,” UBS analysts said. The increased target comes as gold demand reached a record high of 4,974 metric tons in 2024, according to the World Gold Council, which also indicated that central banks' net buying exceeded 1,000 metric tons for the third straight year.

According to Seeking Alpha:

“Goldman Sachs analysts said gold remains its "highest conviction trading recommendation across commodities, driven by structural (Central Bank buying) and cyclical (ETF buying) factors," reiterating a $3,000/oz price forecast for Q2 2026.”

Experts at Trading Economics (see the “Forecast” section on this page of the Trading Economics website) have released average gold price forecasts indicating that the toy ounce will be at $2,939.54/ounce in 12 months

With this outlook for gold prices, investors may want to be exposed to the expected rise in the troy ounce. However, since investing in the physical precious metal directly is not affordable for everyone as it involves a financial commitment that only large investors or banks can leverage, the alternative for smaller investors is to invest in gold stocks listed in the US stock markets.

About Contango Ore, Inc. in Alaska. Its Correlation With Gold Prices

With this article, we shed light on the NYSE-listed shares of Contango Ore, Inc., under the CTGO symbol, as this gold stock has a pronounced positive correlation with the gold price changes after the pouring of the first gold bar in early July 2024.

Contango Ore, Inc. is a Fairbanks, Alaska-based gold producer with a 30% interest in the Peak Gold Joint Venture, which operates the high-grade Manh Choh gold mine on land owned by the Alaska Native Village of Tetlin. Kinross Gold Corporation holds the remaining 70% of the Peak Gold joint venture. Kinross Gold operates the mine and transports the high-grade gold ore from Manh Choh to the processing plant, the Kinross Alaska Mill in Fort Knox, 380 kilometers away.

The Manh Choh mine is expected to produce 225,000 ounces per year of gold equivalent (which also includes silver ounces) over 4.5 years of operation or approximately 1 million equivalent ounces in total. Contango production will be approximately 67,500 gold equivalent ounces per year or a total of 303,750 ounces over 4.5 years of operation.

The correlation between Contango stock price and gold price is shown by a yellow curve in the lower part of the following chart. The correlation is mostly positive as the yellow curve has been above zero for most of the last 12 months. A positive correlation means that when gold prices are higher, Contango shares are most likely trying to reach higher levels as well, which is valuable information, we believe, as gold prices are expected to continue their upward trend.

The correlation recently reversed and turned negative as, while gold continued to rise, Contango shares fell to a four-year low in trading on Friday (November 29, 2024), “after guiding for 25% higher costs at Alaska gold mine.” Seeking Alpha reports.

In our view, Contango Ore's share price will resume its uptrend in line with the price of gold. Before we explain why market demand for Contango Ore's shares will pick up again and trigger a price rally, we will use some current trend indicators to illustrate the first of two components that are paving the way for a strong price rally: the currently very low share price.

The Share Price Is Clearly Low And Very Close To Oversold Levels. Lower Levels Cannot Be Ruled Out At This Time. We Signal To Wait For Now

At the time of writing, Contango Ore shares listed on the NYSE under the symbol CTGO were trading at $10.01 apiece, giving it a market capitalization of $122.41 million.

Shares are trading below the MA Ribbon and very close to the lower boundary of the 52-week range of $9.65/share to $25.32/share.

The 14-day Relative Strength Indicator of 38.36x suggests that shares are very close to oversold levels, which can be considered as a base or support below which shares will not face much pressure but a counter-upside momentum for recovery will instead form.

With inflation data coming in hotter than expected on February 13, markets suspect the Fed will opt for just a 25-basis point rate cut rather than the previously expected two 25-basis point cuts this year. And the rate cut may not come into effect until autumn 2025. A growing belief that the Fed will remain “tight” on rates, which does not bode well for zero-yielding gold, will also negatively impact Contango Ore shares.

Therefore, we recommend a “Hold” for shares of Contango Ore for now.

Contango Ore Will Reverse The Trend and Re-Establish the Positive Correlation with the Rising Gold Price

We expect robust demand for Contango Ore shares.

The positive catalyst will be the profitability of Contango Ore's business, i.e., earnings or cash flow. Since profitability is a key factor for US-listed stocks, the principle is that an improvement in Contango's earnings or cash flow due to the sale of its gold equivalent production along with rising gold prices will regain the market's attention and Contango Ore's shares will ultimately recover well. That is the expectation.

We have already provided insights and forecasts on the topic of gold prices at the beginning of the article. Let's take a look at Contango Ore's business operations and how these, combined with expected rising gold prices, could affect the company's profitability.

Higher Costs Than Originally Expected Are No Reason To Worry

In November, Rick Van Nieuwenhuyse, President and CEO of Contango Ore, Inc., commented on the temporary AISC impact of new weight restrictions on freight at Manh Choh. He said:

“Overall, we are very pleased with the ramp up of Manh Choh production, expecting to produce 38,500 ounces of gold on Contango's account for 2024 and 60,000 ounces of gold for 2025. While we are disappointed with these new weight restrictions impacting Manh Choh trucking operations, which has an impact on AISC, the Manh Choh Project is expected to be very profitable over its current four to five year mine life, generating significant free cash flow to Contango.”

Compared to the US$1,116/oz cited in Manh Choh's 2023 technical report, life-of-mine (“LoM”) All-In-Sustaining Costs (“AISC/oz”) are expected to be higher at US$1,400/oz gold sold and are expected to be US$1,625/oz gold sold in 2025.

It is not possible to go into detail about the causes of higher AISC per ounce compared to the technical document unless these higher cost factors have already occurred in Q3 2024, that is, they have been experienced as part of reality, which is one thing, while a project on a piece of paper or a presentation on a slide is another. Since this assumption is reasonable, we believe that despite the higher-than-expected AISC (which may already be strongly on track to reach $1,625/oz in 2025), Contango Ore managed to hit strong profitability as early as the third quarter of 2024 (subsequent paragraph). The rising price per ounce of the yellow metal was very helpful.

The company achieved an average blended gold price of $2,252 per ounce of gold sold, as 14,825 ounces were sold via hedge contracts at $2,020 per ounce (net) and 12,850 ounces were sold at an average spot price of $2,521/ounce higher than the average market spot price of approx. $2,476.23/ounce in the third quarter of 2024.

Contango Ore Business: Profit and Cash Flow Performance

As a result, net loss has improved dramatically from a net loss of $13.2 million (or -$1.47 per share) in the third quarter of 2023 to a net loss of $9.7 million (-$0.81 per share) in the third quarter of 2024.

The net loss in the third quarter of 2024 is impacted by the charge of an "unrealized" loss on mark-to-market derivative contracts resulting from the comparison between a forward gold price and the contractually agreed hedging price. When the net income in the third quarter of 2024 is adjusted for this non-cash expense of $22.9 million, the result is the green for $9.7 million.

In addition, Contango Ore states that the main reason for the dramatic improvement in net income is that

“the Company's 30% owned, Peak Gold JV, commencing production at Manh Choh resulting in income from its equity investment in the amount of $28.5 M compared to a $5.6 M loss in Q3-2023.”

And with gold prices expected to be well above the $2,500/oz that Contango Ore assumed for its estimate of $50 million in cash flows from the Peak Gold JV in 2025, the stock is well positioned to improve its financial position. Contango can continue the efforts already made in the third quarter of 2024, aggressively deleveraging and continuing its exploration programs at Lucky Shot and Johnson.

These are all developments that the market can only welcome.

There were cash inflows from operating activities of $10.6 million during 9M of 2024 (as of the September 2024 quarter) compared to cash outflows from operating activities of $7.7 million during 9M of 2023 (as of the September 2023 quarter). The company had to draw down credit facilities of $30 million and raise cash proceeds from equity offerings of $15.5 million. Combined with cash inflows from operating activities of $10.6 million (for a total of $56.1 million), there were more than sufficient funds to cover investing activities, which were a total of $31.9 million during 9M of 2024 compared to $38.9 million during 9M of 2023.

Possibly Lower Demand For Credit Facilities Or Equity Issuance Promises A Good Climate For The Stock Price

With the Manh Choh project now producing gold and sales occurring at a significantly higher gold price per ounce, funds from credit lines and equity issuance are likely to be increasingly replaced by cash flow from operations. A more favorable year-on-year trend was already evident as draw-downs on the credit facility decreased from $17.7 million for 9M of 2023 to $15.5 million for 9M of 2024.

Regarding equity issuance, a lower share price undoubtedly impacted the lower amount raised through equity issuance but weighed on the number of shares outstanding (“S.O.”) that needed to be offered in greater quantity to raise sufficient funds: equity issuance decreased from $34.1 million in the first nine months of 2023 to $15.5 million in the first nine months of 2024, but S.O. growth was 2.3 million new shares in the first nine months of 2023 versus 3.2 million new shares in the first nine months of 2024. Improved profitability leads to the possibility of an increase in the share price, and with a higher share price comes a reduced risk of share dilution if the company still wants to raise money through equity issuance. Basically, the markets don't like the idea of ​​a number of common shares that might go up in the public market because a higher count of S.O.s means less earnings per share, which ultimately leads to a deterioration in the market sentiment towards the stock. This is the opposite of what is desired in terms of market sentiment when the company instead uses the funds to buy back its own shares from the public.

A Stronger Financial Condition Will Benefit Further Exploration In Alaska

As of September 30, 2024, Contango Ore had a total of $36.2 million in cash and ST investments on its balance sheet, burdened by $73.9 million of debt. As previously mentioned, the debt is merely a drawn-down credit facility and in the third quarter of 2024, encouraged by improved profitability backed by the first quarter of gold production in Alaska as well as rising gold prices, the company was also able to repay debts of $2 million.

Contango is well positioned to finance its portfolio of gold projects in Alaska, including Lucky Shot, a high-grade underground gold mining project located approximately 180 kilometres north of Anchorage, Alaska, and Johnson Tract, a gold-silver project located approximately 200 kilometres southwest of Anchorage.

Lucky Shot hosts 226,963 tonnes of indicated resources at an average gold grade of 14.5 g/t (grams of gold per tonne of mineral) containing 105,620 ounces of gold and 82,058 tonnes of inferred resources at an average gold grade of 9.5 g/t containing 25,110 ounces of gold.

Johnson Tract hosts 3.49 million tonnes of indicated resources at an average grade of 5.33 grams of gold per tonne, containing 598,000 ounces of gold. The deposit also hosts 673,000 ounces of silver, 400.8 million pounds of zinc, 43.1 million pounds of copper and 51.5 million pounds of lead.

Conclusion

Gold prices are rising, driven by lower interest rates and geopolitical risks that bolster its safe haven qualities. Investors may want to consider Contango Ore, Inc., an Alaska-based company engaged in gold exploration and production at Manh Choh, a high-grade gold project on the land of the Alaska Native Village of Tetlin. Contango has an interest in this project through a joint venture with Kinross Gold Corporation (KGC) (K:CA). Rather than investing directly in physical gold, which is not affordable for everyone, a position in Contango Ore shares is a cheaper and more practical alternative. The company’s profitability is benefiting from rising gold prices and production from the Manh Choh mine in Alaska following the first gold pour in early July 2024. The shares dipped as the company lifted its costs and adopted a gloomier cost outlook than previously published in the technical report. The market didn’t like it, but even at a higher AISC/oz, the likelihood of rising profitability is well protected by the robust prospects for the price of gold and 65% of the sales volume is hedged at a price much higher than increased costs per ounce. The company is well positioned to improve its financials and find new momentum to continue exploration in Alaska and take production beyond 4.5 years. In the short term, some issues could still affect the share price, so investors may want to hold out for a while.

data.title: Fastly: Customer Churn Remains A Major Risk
data.description:
Especially amid a sky-high stock market that is dancing nervously around all-time highs, there is a lot of merit in chasing after “buy the dip” plays, as these stocks may rebound for reasons unrelated to the broader market risks. However, buying falling knives requires very careful stock-picking, as many are value traps that have little to no hope for recovery.

Unfortunately, I believe Fastly (NYSE:FSLY) sits in this unfortunate category. Simply put, the company is in a very messy state: and the stock's ~65% decline over the past year reflects that harsh reality. Having dropped a further ~20% after reporting very weak Q4 results, it's very unlikely for Fastly to be able to recover from its current doldrums.

Though cheap, Fastly's customer losses make it a value trap

I last wrote a bearish opinion on Fastly in November, when the stock was trading closer to $7 per share. Since then, Fastly has enjoyed a bit of a phantom rally in tandem with the rest of the stock market in the post-election euphoria, but its latest results have knocked the momentum out of this run. The company has reported worsening churn metrics, alongside a drop in its base of active customers: With this in mind, I'm reiterating my sell rating on Fastly.

The single biggest issue with Fastly is its declining net revenue retention rates. The company's entire business model relies on "land and expand" - that is, get in the door with a potential customer, and then grow their spending over time. The basic thesis of Fastly and other CDNs (content delivery networks) is that internet usage will continue to rise: and as people watch more and more videos online and access more content, Fastly will earn a greater cut for processing that traffic.

Those secular tailwinds are certainly still there: There's no evidence whatsoever, despite current macro headwinds, that consumers are using the internet and engaging with content any less than they were before. However, sales missteps on Fastly's part are causing its customers to shift their traffic away.

The only saving grace to Fastly: The stock is dirt cheap, especially after the recent correction. At current share prices near $8, Fastly trades at a market cap of $1.12 billion. After we net off the $295.9 million of cash against $337.6 million of debt on Fastly's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $1.16 billion.

Meanwhile, for the current fiscal year FY25, Fastly has guided to $575-$585 million in revenue, representing a paltry growth range of just 6-8% y/y (the company is expecting continued pressure on churn and net retention rates in the first half of the year, but is hoping for a recovery in the back half).

This puts Fastly's valuation at just 2.0x EV/FY25 revenue. This is a bargain in today's market, and it's difficult to imagine now that Fastly had once traded at a double-digit revenue multiple in its heyday during the pandemic, when the stock briefly changed hands at over $100 per share.

However, this cheap valuation reflects a company with core business fundamentals headed the wrong way. Continue to steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Fastly's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Fastly's revenue grew at just a 2% y/y pace to $140.6 million, which beat Wall Street's expectations of $138.7 million (flat y/y) but decelerated sharply from 7% y/y growth in Q4 (and makes us think that the company's goal of re-accelerating to high-single-digit growth in FY25 may be a bit frothy).

A couple of Fastly's core metrics tell the story with more clarity. First, notice in the chart below that Fastly's net revenue retention rates decayed even further to just 102%, from 105% in the prior quarter (and much worse relative to 113% in the prior-year Q4):

The company cited lower spending from its top customers as the culprit here (the company notes that the top 10 customers account for roughly 30% of revenue). Per CEO Todd Nightingale's remarks on the Q4 earnings call detailing the company's sales issues:

As we have discussed in prior quarters, we faced demand headwinds with a few of our largest customers. I'm happy to say we persevered and resumed strength with these larger customers into year-end as we will discuss today. We were also able to grow in other areas to offset these headwinds, and we look to build momentum as we head into 2025 [...]

As we look to 2025, we can expect continued growth and penetration of predictable package revenue as demonstrated by our RPO coming off its lows about a year-ago and growing 4% quarter-over-quarter. We've continued to build our channel presence and our 2024 deal registrations grew 28% year-over-year. I believe that we have far more opportunity here to drive a greater outcome, especially in customer acquisition through deal registration and we plan to do exactly that with greater focus in 2025."

To me, this customer churn is disproving the viability of Fastly's land and expand business model, and may be a signal that customers are defecting to competitors.

You'll also notice that Fastly's customer base dropped sharply from ~3.6k customers at the end of Q3 to just 3,061 at the end of Q4. The company brushed off this decline, noting that it found and subsequently fixed an error in its customer reporting in which a large (unspecified) number of customers altogether generated less than $0.1 million in revenue.

While I agree with Fastly that these customers were an immaterial contributor to its overall results, this loss of customers serves to highlight one of Fastly's key problems: it's having trouble retaining customers. The company encouraged investors to focus on its enterprise customer count, which added 20 net-new customers to end Q4 at 596 total large customers: But even this metric is rocky. In Q3'24, Q1'24, and Q3'23, Fastly lost enterprise customers on a net basis.

The premise of an enterprise software business is that it's permissible to spend huge outlays to attract new customers upfront, since they will generate lifetime revenue in excess of the cost required to attain them. However, when Fastly so regularly switches between customer gains and losses, it highlights the fact that its product is not sticky at all.

On the profitability front, Fastly's pro forma gross margins declined 270bps y/y to 56.5%: Showcasing the dis-economies of scale that Fastly is shouldering due to its greater-than-expected customer churn.

The company's adjusted EBITDA also fell -15% y/y to $9.7 million, representing a 6.9% margin, down 140bps y/y.

Key takeaways

To me, Fastly is not a high-quality "buy the dip" play. The company's churn issues will take time to resolve, and despite management's hope that the second half of FY25 will see stabilization, recent trends suggest that Fastly's actual business reality is much more volatile.

Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Sticky Inflation Is A Quagmire For Tariff Plans
data.description:
By Breakingviews

The U.S. economy is still running too hot for comfort. Prices rose in January by 0.5% from the prior month, nudging the annual increase up to the psychologically testing 3% threshold. It's another roadblock for further rate cuts from the Federal Reserve. More importantly, it leaves even less room for President Donald Trump, whose tariff plans could stoke inflation further. Something must give – raising the specter of recession.

Inflationary pressure is the result of a gaggle of disparate causes: egg prices were up 15% over the prior month; car insurance is nearly 12% more expensive than last year. Sure, prices for new vehicles, apparel, and medical care stayed stable or fell. But from all this noise emerges a strong upward trend: month-over-month price growth has increased progressively since October.

Investors have gotten the message, trimming expectations for any easing of monetary policy – and increasing their bets on renewed tightening. As of last week, markets were pricing in a 10% chance of higher interest rates by the end of 2025. On Wednesday, that rose closer to 20%, according to CME’s FedWatch. Inflation expectations are rising: the University of Michigan’s consumer sentiment survey released on Friday showed the anticipated rise in prices over the next year jumped to 4.3% in February, up from 3.3% in January. Part of this is due to tariffs. Researchers at the Boston Fed estimate that currently paused 25% duties on Mexico and Canada, plus an actually imposed 10% duty on China, would yield an increase of up to 0.8%. Levies on steel and proposed tit-for-tat tariffs would add to the burden.

This is perhaps not the world people in the administration thought they were going to inhabit. Trump promised throughout his campaign to lower prices on his first day in office, with a particular focus on gasoline and eggs, which notched some of the largest cost increases across categories recorded in January. Throttling government spending now seems to be the preferred remedy. Advisor and Tesla boss Elon Musk has declared it the cause of inflation. National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett told CNBC on Monday that lower aggregate demand is needed, a big component of which is the federal budget.

Of course, if consumer demand falters alongside reduced government expenditures, the U.S. would likely fall into a recession, given that upwards of two-thirds of economic activity comes from consumer spending. There are some worrying signs already from corporate America, with Kraft Heinz shares opening 7% lower in early trading on Wednesday after the Oscar Mayer owner said that falling sales were pressured by price increases. Trump would be better off simply dialing back costly plans.

Context News

U.S. inflation rose 3% over the prior year in January, the Bureau of Labor Statistics said on February 12. National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett said on February 10 that to bring inflation down, "you have to increase supply and reduce aggregate demand."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: 25% Tariffs On Steel Make Cleveland-Cliffs A Buy
data.description:
President Trump’s announcement of 25% tariffs on steel and aluminum caused a sharp rally in steel stocks on Monday. Cleveland-Cliffs (NYSE:CLF), Nucor (NUE), and US Steel (X) all had strong gains on the day. Cleveland-Cliffs posted the biggest gain of the three, up 18%.

Steel as a commodity had a tough year in 2024, dropping 17.6% YOY. US Steel was the object of a bidding war, as Cleveland-Cliffs CEO Lourenco Goncalves offered to buy the company in late 2023. Nippon steel, among others, started a bidding war in which Nippon steel won out with a $54 a share offer. Ironically, that was $1 a share more than what Cliffs bid. US steel elected to go with Nippon steel for an extra $253 million, which turned out to be a big mistake.

Both President Biden and then candidate Trump vowed to block the deal due to national security issues. Another major concern was the union, who was in full support of Cliffs and their CEO. Lourenco Goncalves is a friend of the steelworkers union and has a strong relationship with the chief, David McCall. Cliffs had the full backing of the union to be the one company to buy US Steel. The deal between Nippon and US Steel was delayed by the Biden administration and ultimately blocked.

Cliff goes a different direction and takes over Stelco Steel

CEO Goncalves led Cliffs to purchase Stelco Steel, Canada’s largest steel producer. The deal closed very smoothly late in the third quarter for $2.4 billion. Stelco is a rock solid company with positive cash flow that will be immediately accretive to the bottom line. Mr. Goncalves is well respected and a man of integrity that is loved by the unions. He is a no nonsense guy and a great steel man as well as an outstanding CEO. He is one of my all-time favorite CEO’s to listen to on earnings calls. Furthermore, he has transformed Cleveland-Cliffs into a powerhouse that is now poised for growth as America renews its commitment to prosperity under Trump’s pro-American policy.

Cleveland-Cliffs Inc. stands as a behemoth in the iron ore and steel industry, boasting a vertically integrated business model that spans from mining and pelletizing to steelmaking and finishing. The company's strategic acquisitions and operational efficiencies have positioned it as a key player in the North American market.

Key Strengths

  • Vertical Integration: Cliffs' vertical integration provides it with a stable supply of raw materials and allows it to control costs throughout the production process.

  • Market Leadership: The company's market leadership position gives it significant pricing power and bargaining leverage with customers and suppliers. They are the number one supplier to the automotive industry.

  • Operational Efficiency: Cleveland-Cliffs has a strong track record of operational efficiency, which has helped it to maintain profitability even during challenging market conditions.

Growth Opportunities

  • Infrastructure Spending: Increased infrastructure spending under Trump in the United States is expected to boost demand for steel and iron ore, which could benefit Cliffs.

  • Automotive Sector Recovery: A recovery in the automotive sector could also drive demand for steel, as the automotive industry is a major consumer of steel products. Cliffs is the number one supplier of Tesla.

  • Expansion into New Markets: Cliffs could also explore opportunities to expand into new markets, such as Asia and Europe.

  • Rebuilding Ukraine and Gaza with steel is a distinct possibility with Trump's leadership.
  • The repurposed state of the art electrical transformer plant in Weirton will bring significant new revenue stream online soon.

Risks

  • Cyclical Nature of the Steel Industry: The steel industry is cyclical, and Cliffs' financial performance is sensitive to changes in steel prices and demand.

  • Rising Costs: Rising costs for raw materials, energy, and labor could pressure Cliffs' margins. Cliffs has less exposure to price fluctuations as they source their stock internally.

A look at the charts.

Chart by vectorvest

As one can see from the chart above Cliffs has been in a downtrend for about a year. The stock bottomed at $10.21 on September 11 and then rallied 35% before falling to $11.35 on Nov 5th earnings release.

When President Trump won the election on November 6th, Cliffs gapped up and rallied over 20% in a day to $14.33 before rolling over. Weak steel prices and feeble demand caused the sector to stumble into year-end, with Cliffs putting in a major bottom of $8.99 in late December. An important note: Cliffs is currently trading at a November 2020 low.

Tariffs a game changer

A new uptrend appears to be in the early stages, the catalyst is a pro-America plan and 25% tariffs on steel. Nucor (NUE) and US Steel (X) stand to benefit with tariffs and the America first agenda; however, Cliffs is the most beaten down steel name in the industry. In my opinion, Cliffs is poised for the largest percentage gains over the next 6 to 9 months. CEO Goncalves is integrating Stelco into the business as well as squeezing out costs. He is focused on making Cliffs a powerhouse.

Financial Performance

Cleveland-Cliffs has delivered strong financial performance in recent years, driven by higher steel prices and improved operational efficiency. The company has also reduced its debt levels and strengthened its balance sheet. That said, 2024 was the worst year for steel since the pandemic, according to Goncalves.

For the third quarter of 2024, Cliffs reported a loss of $.52 a share. Consolidated revenues were $4.6 billion, compared to $5.1 billion in the second quarter of 2024.on slow demand from the auto sector. This company posted $124 million of EBITDA, which was lower by $199 million from Q2.

Here is a clip from the 3rd quarter call.

Cliffs also suffered from the dumping of pig iron onto the market. One of the problems for the steel industry is countries backdooring their products through Mexico. Trump will not stand for steel dumping, hence the tariffs. I believe these tariffs will stay in place to protect American steel and jobs.

Cleveland-Cliffs Provides Preliminary Fourth-Quarter and Full-Year 2024 Results

On February 3rd, Cliffs pre-released worse than expected results, with final earning to be reported on the conference call on February 24th. Below is a clip from Cliffs' website.

Fourth-quarter 2024 results expectations:

  • Steel shipments of 3.8 million net tons
  • Revenues of approximately $4.3 billion
  • Adjusted EBITDA loss of approximately $85 million

Full-year 2024 results expectations:

  • Steel shipments of 15.6 million net tons
  • Revenues of approximately $19.2 billion
  • Adjusted EBITDA of approximately $775 million
  • Including Stelco, 2024 Pro-Forma Adjusted EBITDA of approximately $1.2 billion

Lourenco Goncalves, Cliffs’ Chairman, President, and CEO said: “Other than the COVID-impacted 2020, 2024 was the worst year for domestic steel demand since 2010. As the largest supplier to the automotive industry in North America, we were especially impacted by muted demand from this sector in the second half of the year. This was the primary driver of our weaker results, particularly in the fourth quarter, which we expect to be the trough as we look forward. So far into this new year, we have already seen improvements in our order book, both automotive and non-automotive, and are confident that the manufacturing-friendly items on President Trump’s agenda will have an outsized benefit on Cleveland-Cliffs. This includes the recently announced tariffs on Mexico, Canada, and China and the expectation that there is more to come on steel specifically. Stelco has been a major contributor since day 1 and a substantial portion of our expected synergies are already in motion. Based on their experience in 2018, we expect Stelco will benefit from steel tariffs as well. We look forward to the success in 2025 that all of these developments will ultimately bring.”

I believe that the pro-forma $1.3 billion in adjusted EBITDA for 2024 is going to rise over the next 12 to 18 months as steel prices recover. Goncalves estimated an increase of $600 million EBITDA for 2025 based on Stelco deal alone. Steel prices are on the rise and hot-rolled steel recently priced at $765 a ton according to trading economics.com.

I rate Cliffs a buy with an $18 year-end price target. With a market cap of $5.3 billion, Cliffs appears to be significantly undervalued. As one can see by the chart below, it is trading well below book value of $14.64.

Source: stockanalysis.com

Important note: Cliffs just raised $850 million last week, so these numbers are not currently reflected in the balance sheet. You can read about it here.

Cliffs was trading at $14 in late August, and I believe it is going back to that number in the near future as US policy favors steel production here in America. However, investing in the steel industry is not without risk. Economic recession, automotive decline, or continued weakness in steel prices should be factored into risk premium.

Positive development in the works

Cliffs is in the process of repurposing the Weirton tin mill to a state of the art electrical transformer production plant in West Virginia. Interested investors can read about it by clicking here.

President Trump announced today that the US will be needing more than double our electric capacity, and this works into Cliffs' plan to build high-end transformers. The company expects to be online in the first quarter of 2026. This will be another significant revenue stream for the company and provide hundreds of jobs. The lead time is over 8 months for high-end transformers, and the demand is expected to be robust for many years in the future.

Conclusion

The announcement of 25% tariffs on steel and aluminum make steel stocks a worthy investment. The new administration is much more friendly to business and will crack down on illegal dumping. Cleveland-Cliffs is fully integrated and ready to capitalize on the return of manufacturing and the need for transformers for the energy grid.

The CEO, Lourenco Goncalves is one of the best in the industry and has the leadership and integrity to drive Cliffs forward while preserving good union jobs.

Cleveland-Cliffs is a well-positioned company with a strong track record of success. The company's vertical integration, market leadership, and operational efficiency give it a competitive advantage. While there are risks associated with investing in the steel industry, Cliffs' growth opportunities and attractive valuation make it a compelling investment consideration for investors seeking exposure to the steel sector.

Al always I caution everyone to do their own research and make their own decisions and always have an exit point in mind before buying any stock.

data.title: VDY: There Is A Better Canadian Dividend Option In XDIV
data.description:
All figures are in $CAD unless otherwise stated.

All financial information comes from Seeking Alpha unless otherwise stated.

VDY Not The Best In The Strategy

Over the past 5 years, the Vanguard FTSE Canadian High Dividend Yield Index ETF (TSX:VDY:CA) has outperformed all of the larger ETFs with a similar mandate. Over that same period, VDY has also outperformed the SPTSX.

As many of us know, past performance does not guarantee future returns and we believe that to be the case for VDY. While we expect that VDY will track the SPTSX quite closely going forward, we believe there are better options with a similar mandate (i.e. High Yield Canadian Dividend Stocks) due to VDY's poor overall diversification and ETFs with a lower MER. More specifically, we believe XDIV:CA is the better option.

Portfolio Diversification is Misleading

The largest ten holdings in VDY make up 72% of the ETFs holdings as of December 31, 2024. In contrast, XDIVs top ten holdings made up 80% of the weight. At first glance, it actually appears as if VDY is more diversified than XDIV and this metric shows up again with the number of holdings. VDY has 56 holdings and XDIV only has 20. VDY's lack of diversification becomes more prominent when you look at its largest holding, historical risk metrics, and sector exposures.

While VDY has 56 holdings, the largest holding is 16.03% of the ETF (RY:CA) whereas XDIV's largest holding with only 20 holdings is 9.69% (TD:CA). With 56 holdings in VDY and 72% being in the top ten, there is only 28% of weight left to distribute across 46 holdings. In fact, VDY's two largest holdings (2nd largest holding is TD:CA) make up 25% of the weight which means the largest two holdings are almost equal in contribution to the price return as the smallest 46 holdings. Ironically, the other pitfall we would expect from this strategy is higher trading costs and turnover as there are more holdings however, we believe it's important to note that XDIV actually has a higher turnover ratio and trading expense.

Standard deviation (or volatility) measures how much an investment's returns fluctuate. That is, a higher standard deviation means more ups and more downs. The Sharpe Ratio tells you whether or not an investment's returns are worth the risk as it compares the return to volatility. That is, a higher Sharpe ratio means you're getting more return for each unit of risk or a better 'risk-adjusted' performance. The upside capture ratio measures how much of the market's gains an investment captures and the downside capture ratio measures how much of the market's losses an investment captures. That is, you want an upside capture ratio of greater than 100 and a downside capture ratio of less than 100. For example, if the downside capture ratio for VDY is 80, when the market falls 10%, VDY would theoretically only fall 8%. Over the past 3 years, the table below summarizes these key performance/risk metrics for VDY and XDIV:

ETF Volatility Sharpe Ratio Upside Capture Downside Capture
VDY 13.58% 0.36 95 108
XDIV 11.56% 0.62 86 77

What we quickly notice is that XDIV has offered much better risk-adjusted performance and when the market is 'down', XDIV does a much better job insulating losses. However, in an 'up' market, VDY does a better job capitalizing on gains. We highlight the importance of these metrics because we believe that many investors who utilize these ETFs as a part of their portfolio are retirees looking for dividends to supplement their lifestyle. In order to preserve/grow your wealth, you should want something that is going to have reasonable capital growth, high dividend yield, and relatively low volatility. We believe that XDIV is the better option here as VDY and XDIV are essentially interchangeable from a capital growth perspective, XDIV has a higher forward dividend yield of 4.24% versus VDY at 3.97% (growth rates in dividends are also very similar - data is from Seeking Alpha), and XDIV has historically offered lower risk as seen above.

Finally, we turn our attention to the sector exposures (data from each respective ETF provider as at December 31, 2024) which can be seen in the table below:

Sector VDY XDIV Difference
Financials 58.54% 45.80% 12.74%
Energy 29.64% 33.27% -3.63%
Utilities 5.08% 11.66% -6.58%
Telecommunications 4.37% 3.98% 0.39%
Basic Materials 1.01% 0.00% 1.01%
Consumer Cyclical 0.62% 5.29% -4.67%
Real Estate 0.27% 0.00% 0.27%
Industrials 0.26% 0.00% 0.26%
Consumer Defensive 0.17% 0.00% 0.17%
Technology 0.05% 0.00% 0.05%
Healthcare 0.00% 0.00% 0.00%
Other 0.00% 0.00% 0.00%

As seen in the table above, VDY has a much larger exposure to the Financials sector followed by slightly larger exposures in Basic Materials and Telecommunications. Where VDY is underweight compared to XDIV is Energy, Utilities, and Consumer Cyclical Stocks. Our attention is mainly geared at the near 60% exposure of VDY to the financials sector. If the Financial sector were to experience a recession (e.g. high levels of default, slower capital markets), VDY would be heavily exposed. We note that XDIV isn't much better from this point of view however, having higher exposure to other sectors that aren't as correlated with the market (i.e. Energy) or sectors that are viewed as less risky (i.e. Utilities), somewhat makes up for this pitfall.

With all of the information presented above, we believe XDIV is less risky for investors and offers retirees who look for this mandate a better path to preserving their wealth.

So Who's Management Expense Ratio is Better?

VDY currently has an MER of 0.22% and XDIV currently has an MER of 0.11%. Assuming an investment in each of $10,000.00, a 5% annual return, and a 10-year holding period, you could expect to pay VDY $280 in management fees whereas with the same assumptions, you could expect to pay $141 in management fees. This may not seem like much at first but with a sizeable investment, these differences add up and when we consider the points we mentioned above, we would prefer to pay for the lower fee ETF. Not to mention, both of these ETFs essentially replicate the performance of the overall Canadian stock market so a lower MER in our opinion is worth it.

Finally, while VDY is the larger fund with $3.4 billion in total assets (XDIV has $2 billion), RBC iShares has more AUM than Vanguard and by investing XDIV, you are supporting a Canadian corporation versus an American corporation (not that this matters, we thought it was something fun to point out).

Conclusion

In summary, we believe that XDIV is the better option for retirees looking at VDY for their next investment as it appears to be the safer choice and investors can sleep well at night knowing they are getting one of the lowest MERs in the country for this mandate. This article is not an opposition against VDY as we believe the ETF can fit nicely into the right person's portfolio. We feel that XDIV is just the better option for a larger set of investors.

data.title: Nvidia: A Buy For Bold Investors Despite DeepSeek's Cost Claims
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation's (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:NVDA) stock has stalled over the last several months due to valuation concerns, making the stock sensitive to the market selling off the stock if the company's high revenue growth and profitability come under an actual or perceived threat. An example of a perceived threat to revenue growth was when DeepSeek's (DEEPSEEK) news of training an AI model at lower costs than OpenAI's model hit the market, opening up the possibility that demand could fall for Nvidia's powerful AI chip. The market's immediate reaction was to sell off the stock. It dropped around 17% on January 27. While it has recovered some since then, the DeepSeek news continues to weigh down the stock.

Before the DeepSeek news hit the market, I upgraded NVDA stock to a Buy rating for only aggressive growth investors on December 10, 2024. Although the stock's high valuation and risks are genuine, I reasoned that the market may undervalue the company's estimated +50% revenue and earnings growth rate in its fiscal year (“FY”) 2026. However, this buy rating is only appropriate for investors willing to accept the stock's significant potential downside if the company misses quarterly results expectations or the long-term technological threat of customers reducing their reliance on Nvidia's increasingly powerful AI chips becomes more immediate.

This article will discuss the DeepSeek threat and why it's not a concern for Nvidia over the near and medium term. It will examine its third-quarter earnings call and what to expect when the company reports fourth-quarter results. The article will also review Nvidia's long-term risks and valuation, and why it is a buy for aggressive growth investors willing to speculate on the AI infrastructure market.

The DeepSeek Threat

It is difficult to question Nvidia's short- and medium-term dominance because the AI chip market is supply constrained. However, once DeepSeek came onto the scene, claiming that it could train AI models at substantially lower costs, it exposed a latent fear among some Nvidia bulls that technological advances in AI models could make increasing the raw computing power in chips less necessary over the long term. Customers could shift to chips that are powerful enough but consume less energy, which may neuter some of Nvidia's head start in creating increasingly more powerful AI chips.

Before I go further into this discussion, it's essential to understand that the conversation around DeepSeek can be confusing for a non-technical person. I had to revise this discussion about DeepSeek several times because I discovered that some articles about the topic have some misconceptions. The first thing to understand is that there are three steps in training an AI model:

  • Pretraining (before the model use)
  • Post-training (after the models use)
  • Test-time scaling (during the inferencing use of the model).

I will explain what pretraining an AI model entails later in this article, but it is likely the most expensive step. Post-training is like fine-tuning the AI model for specific topics or use cases, and is less costly because training datasets are smaller and training takes less time than pretraining. Test-time scaling takes place during inferencing, the time the AI model is generating a response to a prompt. Test-time scaling is the lowest cost out of all three training steps.

A few initial articles discussing how DeepSeek lowered costs focused on pretraining costs. For instance, Bain and Company recently published an article that stated:

What sets DeepSeek apart is the prospect of radical cost efficiency. The company claims to have trained its model for just $6 million using 2,000 Nvidia H800 graphics processing units ("GPUs") vs. the $80 million to $100 million cost of GPT-4 and the 16,000 H100 GPUs required for Meta's LLaMA 3​. While the comparisons are far from apples to apples, the possibilities are valuable to understand.

This statement alarmed some people because if DeepSeek's claims are valid, and it is possible to create models the equivalent of GPT-4 or LLaMa 3 at far less cost with much fewer GPUs, that could, in the long term, create far less demand for NVIDIA's highest-end GPUs. It also raises the possibility that companies could train and inference their AI models on more price-performant (good enough performance at a lower cost) AI chips, such as Amazon's (AMZN) Trainum chips. Amazon's Chief Executive Officer Andy Jassy said on the company's recent fourth quarter 2024 earnings call:

Trainium 2 just launched at our AWS Reinvent Conference in December and EC2 instances with these chips are typically 30% to 40% more price performant than other current GPU-powered instances available. That's very compelling at scale. Several technically capable companies like Adobe (ADBE), Databricks, poolside, and Qualcomm (QCOM) have seen impressive results in early testing of Trainium 2. It's also why you're seeing Anthropic build their future frontier models on Trainium 2. We're collaborating with Anthropic to build Project Rainier, a cluster of Trainium 2 ultra servers containing hundreds of thousands of Trainium 2 chips. This cluster is going to be 5 times the number of exaflops as the cluster that Anthropic used to train their current leading set of cloud models.

Nvidia will likely not see any adverse impact from competition in a supply constrained AI chip market. However, over the long term, as supply and demand balance out, companies like Marvell (MRVL), Broadcom (AVGO), and others building increasingly more price-performant chips may make it more challenging for Nvidia to grow at the rates investors require to justify its valuation — or at least that is one bearish theory.

Don't underestimate Nvidia

The need for Nvidia to create increasingly more powerful chips may not diminish for quite a while, if ever. While DeepSeek may have developed an innovative AI model, the company's cost-efficiency claims for pretraining an AI model may be far less than those cited in the Bain news article. Semianalysis published a news article on January 31, 2025, that stated:

DeepSeek's price and efficiencies caused the frenzy this week, with the main headline being the "$6M" dollar figure training cost of DeepSeek V3. This is wrong. This akin to pointing to a specific part of a bill of materials for a product and attributing it as the entire cost. The pretraining cost is a very narrow portion of the total cost...The $6M cost in the paper is attributed to just the GPU cost of the pretraining run, which is only a portion of the total cost of the model. Excluded are important pieces of the puzzle like R&D and TCO of the hardware itself. For reference, Claude 3.5 Sonnet cost $10s of millions to train, and if that was the total cost Anthropic needed, then they would not raise billions from Google and tens of billions from Amazon. It's because they have to experiment, come up with new architectures, gather and clean data, pay employees, and much more.

Pretraining a large language model (“LLM”) involves feeding the model massive datasets consisting of publicly accessible text online, such as books, articles, web pages, code, and video transcripts, to learn language patterns. The costs for pretraining consist of money spent on hardware (GPUs, memory, and networking equipment), data acquisition and cleaning, AI and Data engineer employee expenses, Research and Development (R&D), and operational costs such as server maintenance and utility costs. What the above statement implies is that DeepSeek only included the costs of buying or renting the GPU and possibly the utility costs of using the GPU. It excluded R&D expenses, data acquisition and cleaning costs, labor, and ongoing expenses related to server maintenance, API usage, and infrastructure scaling (adding more computing, storage, and networking resources). In short, the above commentary means the overall costs for training an AI model involve more than just GPU costs, and the $6 million figure may be deceptive.

OpenAI released a full version of its latest AI model, GPT-o1, on December 05, 2024. DeepSeek released its latest R-1 model on January 20, 2025. I haven't found a communication from OpenAI outlining how much it costs to develop GPT-o1, so it is difficult to compare developmental costs between DeepSeek R-1 and OpenAI GPT-o1. However, I wouldn't be surprised if the total cost to create the DeepSeek-R1 model and GpT-o1 are similar.

On the day the DeepSeek news roiled the markets, Seeking Alpha published an article that stated,

“'DeepSeek is an excellent AI advancement and a perfect example of Test Time Scaling,' an Nvidia spokesperson told Seeking Alpha in response to the stock market selloff.”

Test Time Scaling is a new AI paradigm that adds extra computing power at the point the AI model generates text and images or makes a prediction. This additional computing power gives the AI more time to “think” or reason, theoretically leading to better answers. More “thinking time” may require more powerful AI chips, meaning that Test Time Scaling could lead to higher demand for Nvidia's chips over price-performant chips. Theoretically, the AI may take longer to respond if price-performant chips cannot provide the same inferencing power as NVIDIA's chips. Depending on an organization's need for its AI to respond quickly, it still may want to use Nvidia's chips over Amazon's Trainium chips, for instance.

Going deeper down the rabbit hole, both R-1 and GPT-o1 use Time Test Scaling when inferencing. The comparison between the two AI models when responding to questions may be the primary reason people are interested in DeepSeek, not the pretraining costs. Seeking Alpha discourages linking to specific YouTube videos, so I will refrain from doing so here. However, if you want to see a few comparisons of the two AI models answering questions, Google “GPT-01 versus DeepSeek comparison,” and several videos will pop up.

One big difference between the two AI models is that DeepSeek's models are free and open source. In contrast, GPT-o1 is only available to premium users of ChatGPT for $20 a month for limited service and $200 for unlimited service through ChatGPT pro plan. Be aware that the o1 model through the ChatGPT pro plan is slightly more advanced than the AI model on the $20 limited service. This free versus open-source battle illustrates how open-source models could potentially hurt companies using an AI-as-a-service business model.

In response to questions, R-1 appears to have a more extensive reasoning process for generating an answer. The $20/month plan GPTo1 service typically has a shorter reasoning process behind obtaining a final answer than R-1. In the videos that I have watched, GPT-o1 ($20/month version) responds (“thinks”) faster, but occasionally gets difficult questions wrong. The $200 version of GPT-o1 answered the questions correctly that the $20 of GPT-o1 got wrong. The free R-1 and the $200 GPT-o1 appear to be equivalent services. However, there are reasons enterprises would choose GPT-o1 over R-1. KrebsonSecurity recently published an article that stated:

New mobile apps from the Chinese artificial intelligence ((AI)) company DeepSeek have remained among the top three "free" downloads for Apple and Google devices since their debut on January 25, 2025. But experts caution that many of DeepSeek's design choices — such as using hard-coded encryption keys, and sending unencrypted user and device data to Chinese companies — introduce a number of glaring security and privacy risks... DeepSeek's rapid rise caught the attention of the mobile security firm NowSecure, a Chicago-based company that helps clients screen mobile apps for security and privacy threats. In a teardown of the DeepSeek app published today, NowSecure urged organizations to remove the DeepSeek iOS mobile app from their environments, citing security concerns.

I don't think DeepSeek will ever be a serious threat to OpenAI, Microsoft (MSFT), or Alphabet (GOOGL, GOOG) because it's unlikely a Western company will trust a Chinese company's technology with sensitive information. However, DeepSeek has opened up the path for open-source companies from Western nations, possibly introducing an app that could threaten the AI-as-a-Service business model.

What about Nvidia? If you believe the company's management team, Test Time Scaling will only increase demand for Nvidia's more powerful AI chips in the inference phase over more price-performant chips. Additionally, AI models are becoming increasingly more complex. Chief Executive Officer Jensen Huang said on the company's third-quarter earnings call:

On the other hand, the models are getting larger, they're multimodality. Just the number of dimensions that inference is innovating is incredible. And this innovation rate is what makes Nvidia's architecture so great because our ecosystem is fantastic. Everybody knows that if they innovate on top of CUDA on top of Nvidia's architecture, they can innovate more quickly and they know that everything should work. And if something were to happen, it's probably likely their code and not ours. And so that ability to innovate in every single direction at the same time, having a large installed base so that whatever you create could land on a Nvidia computer and be deployed broadly all around the world in every single data center all the way out to the edge into robotic systems, that capability is really quite phenomenal.

CEO Huang alludes to several advantages in the statement above that help keep it at the top of the AI chip market, including the CUDA platform, the ecosystem, developer tools, software, customer support, reliability, and a massive performance advantage over nearest competitors. A Seeking Alpha article quoting Evercore ISI analyst Mark Lipacis stated:

Nvidia is still the platform of choice for hyperscalers' customers, with it being 5 to 10 years ahead of "anything else in the market," Lipacis added. AMD (AMD) and Amazon Web Services are seen as a "distant" second and third, Lipacis explained. And while application specific integrated circuits [price-performant chips] will have a role, external workloads (such as cloud facing AWS, Google Cloud and Azure), and enterprise on-premise will likely remain dominated by Nvidia, Lipacis said.

The company also has several other defenses against a future where companies may shift away from more powerful chips toward more price-performant chips. For instance, Nvidia is a free cash flow (“FCF”) machine today, and it can invest heavily in developing price-performant chips if it sees that's the direction in which the market is evolving.

Nvidia may have started as a hardware company that manufactured GPUs. However, it is evolving into a company that provides end-to-end AI solutions. It gives customers software tools to build chatbots, AI virtual assistants, and virtual agents and now sells cloud services alongside the company's high-performance AI chips. So, it's more than just a chip provider—it's a full-fledged AI company.

It also emphasizes the Total Cost of Ownership (“TCO”) over its entire AI infrastructure solution rather than focusing solely on making the chip, which provides a powerful defense against competitors only selling price-performant chips. NVIDIA includes the whole hardware and software ecosystem, support, operating expenses, and the ability to deploy AI solutions rapidly into its TCO calculations. Management wants to be able to go to a customer and say, “Our AI chips may cost more upfront, but our whole AI solution saves money over the long term.”

So, although DeepSeek and momentum towards driving AI costs lower threaten Nvidia in the long term, it has ways of fighting back, and whether this risk will manifest adversely in the company's business remains highly uncertain.

Company fundamentals

Outstanding Hopper sales during the third quarter helped the Data Center segment revenue increase 17% sequentially and 112% year-on-year to $30.8 billion. Hopper is the name of a specific Nvidia GPU architecture family, and H200 is the name of its most advanced model within the Hopper family. Chief Financial Officer (“CFO”) Colette Kress said on the company's third quarter FY 2025 earnings call (emphasis added):

Sequentially, Nvidia H200 sales increased significantly to double-digit billions, the fastest product ramp in our company's history. The H200 delivers up to 2 times faster inference performance and up to 50% improved TCO. Cloud service providers [CSPs] were approximately half of our data center sales with revenue increasing more than 2 times year-on-year. CSPs deployed Nvidia H200 infrastructure and high-speed networking with installations scaling to tens of thousands of GPUs to grow their business and serve rapidly rising demand for AI training and inference workloads. Nvidia H200-powered cloud instances are now available from AWS, CoreWeave, and Microsoft Azure with Google Cloud and OCI [Oracle (ORCL) Cloud Infrastructure] coming soon. Alongside significant growth from our large CSPs, Nvidia GPU regional cloud revenue jumped 2 times year-on-year as North America, EMEA, and Asia Pacific regions ramped Nvidia cloud instances and sovereign cloud buildout.

Outside CSPs, it more than doubled its revenue from consumer internet companies buying its Hopper technology to power training next-generation AI models, multimodal and agentic AI, deep learning recommender engines, generative AI, and content creation. CFO Kress also said on the earnings call (emphasis added):

“Nvidia's Ampere [the architectural family before Hopper] and Hopper infrastructures are fueling inference revenue growth for customers. NVIDIA is the largest inference platform in the world.”

Management likely pointed out that Nvidia has the largest inference platform because that's where it is most vulnerable to losing market share in the long term to competitors with price-performant chips.

CFO Kress said on the earnings call that it shipped its first Blackwell samples to customers during the third quarter. Blackwell is the company's latest architectural family and is in high demand because it's the most powerful chip on the market today. Blackwell can dramatically reduce the training time of large AI models that once took weeks or even months on hardware from two or three years ago. In the rapidly evolving AI industry, the faster developers can bring their innovations to market, the better their chance of succeeding.

The CFO said on the earnings call:

Blackwell is now in the hands of all of our major partners and they are working to bring up their Data Centers. We are integrating Blackwell systems into the diverse Data Center configurations of our customers. Blackwell demand is staggering and we are racing to scale supply to meet the incredible demand customers are placing on us.

Investors can expect Blackwell to drive significant Data Center revenue growth over the next year.

Gaming revenue increased 14% sequentially and 15% year over year to $3.3 billion. Automotive revenue increased 30% sequentially and 72% year-over-year to $449 million. Professional Visualization revenue increased 7% sequentially and 17% year over year to $486 million.

Nvidia's third-quarter FY 2025 total revenue increased 17% sequentially and 94% year-over-year to $35.1 billion, above the company's guidance of $32.5 billion and beating analysts' estimates by $1.95 billion.

Nvidia's GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) expanded 60 basis points (“bps”) year over year to 74.6% due to a higher mix of higher-margin Data Center revenue. Non-GAAP gross margins remained flat year over year at 75%. However, GAAP and non-GAAP gross margins declined sequentially by 50 bps and 70 bps, respectively. A mix shift from mature H100 systems to the newer, more complex H200 systems was primarily responsible for lower sequential gross margins. When ramping up new hardware, gross margins typically decline until the company refines its manufacturing processes and supply chain.

The company's third quarter FY 2025 GAAP operating income increased 110% over the previous year's comparable quarter to $21.869 billion. GAAP operating margin expanded by 484 bps year over year to 62.33%. Its non-GAAP operating income increased 101% year over year to $23.276 billion. The non-GAAP operating margin was 66.34%, which was flat year over year.

Nvidia's GAAP and non-GAAP net income were $19.309 billion and $20.010 billion, respectively. It grew GAAP diluted earnings-per-share (“EPS”) by 109% to $0.78, beating analysts' estimates by $0.08. Non-GAAP diluted EPS grew by 103% year over year to $0.81, beating analysts' estimates by $0.06.

The company ended the third quarter with cash and marketable securities of $38.49 billion and long-term debt of $8.46 billion. Nvidia's trailing 12-month (“TTM”) cash from operations (“CFO”) was 52.05%, which means that for every $1 in sales, the company generates $0.52 in CFO—extremely impressive.

Nvidia's third-quarter FY 2025 CFO increased 22% sequentially and 140% year over year to $17.63 billion. TTM CFO was $58.96 billion.

The company's third quarter FY capital expenditures (“CapEx”) increased 192% year over year to $813 million, and TTM CapEx increased 82.25% year over year to $2.413 billion. Although Nvidia doesn't manufacture the chips it designs, it has high CapEx needs because it has evolved into an AI infrastructure company. CFO Colette Kress said on the earnings call,

“Our investments include building data centers for development of our hardware and software stacks and to support new introductions.”

As a result, Nvidia has more CapEx than a typical fabless semiconductor manufacturer.

Nvidia's third-quarter FY 2025 FCF grew 138% year over year to $16.814 billion. TTM FCF grew 223% year over year to $56.55 billion.

Management used some of its cash flow to repurchase $11.0 billion in shares and pay $245 million in cash dividends.

Management forecasts total revenue of $37.5 billion in the fourth quarter of FY 2025. If it meets this guidance, fourth-quarter revenue will rise 69.7% year over year, and full-year FY 2025 revenue will increase 111% over FY 2024 to $128.66 billion. Nvidia CFO Kress said the following about revenue guidance:

[Revenue guidance] incorporates continued demand for Hopper architecture and the initial ramp of our Blackwell products, while demand is greatly exceed supply, we are on track to exceed our previous Blackwell revenue estimate of several billion dollars as our visibility into supply continues to increase. On gaming, although sell-through was strong in Q3, we expect fourth-quarter revenue to decline sequentially due to supply constraints.

Management forecasts fourth quarter FY 2025 GAAP gross margins of 73.0% and non-GAAP gross margins of 73.5%. This guidance is well below the company's peak GAAP gross margins of 78.4% and non-GAAP gross margins of 78.9% in the first quarter of FY 2025. On the earnings call, CFO Kress stated the reason for the gross margin decline:

As Blackwell ramps, we expect gross margins to moderate to the low-70s. When fully ramped, we expect Blackwell margins to be in the mid-70s.

Later in the earnings call, analysts asked several questions about the Blackwell ramp and how long it would be before gross margins rebounded. Colette Kress responded by saying:

Could we reach the mid-70s in the second half of next year [Calendar year 2026]? And yes, I think it is reasonable assumption or a goal for us to do, but we'll just have to see how that mix of ramp goes. But yes, it is definitely possible.

Analysts are highly interested in when gross margins will rebound because this can impact FCF and EPS, the numbers people use to value the stock. A prolonged decline in gross margins could adversely affect investors' valuation of the stock.

When the company reports its fourth-quarter results on February 26, note what management reports for the first quarter FY 2026 guidance. Currently, consensus analyst revenue estimates are for $41.94 billion. If the company's guidance exceeds those revenue estimates, the market will likely take it as a sign that the DeepSeek news was a non-event. If management's first-quarter guidance comes under analysts' estimates, concerns about DeepSeek could resurface.

Valuation

Nvidia's price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 28.92. With the significant decline in the company's market cap over the last several months, it trades closer to its three-year median, and some may consider it fairly valued. Yet others may believe the market still overvalues the stock based on where it trades above its five-, seven-, and ten-year median.

The company's price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio is 52.44, which is well above the information technology (“IT”) sector's median P/E of 32.63, which may lead some to consider it overvalued. However, some may think the market undervalues it, based on the stock's P/E trading at 38.18% below its five-year average P/E.

Nvidia's one-year forward P/E-to-growth is 0.59 (One-year forward P/E of 29.90 divided by analysts' estimated EPS growth of 50.43%). Typically, growth investors will allow a stock to reach a PEG ratio of 2.0 before considering it overvalued. According to that convention, the market may vastly undervalue Nvidia's EPS growth over the next year. If its PEG ratio were 1.0, the stock price would be $223.91, a 71% rise above its February 12 closing stock price of $131.14. If its PEG ratio were 2.0, the stock price would be $447.81.

Let's analyze Nvidia using a reverse discount cash flow (“DCF”). This DCF uses a terminal growth rate of 4% because the company should continue steadily growing its cash flow well above the market average after the ten-year analysis period. I use a discount rate of 9%, which is the opportunity cost of investing in Nvidia, reflecting a less-than-moderate risk level. This reverse DCF uses an unlevered FCF for the following analysis.

Reverse DCF

The third quarter of FY 2025 reported Free Cash Flow TTM

 $56,550
Terminal growth rate 4%
Discount Rate 9%
Years 1-10 growth rate 17.9%
Stock Price (February 12, 2024, closing price) $131.14
Terminal FCF value $301.180 billion
Discounted Terminal Value $2313.120 billion
FCF margin 49.92%

If Nvidia can maintain a 50% FCF margin for the next ten years, the company must grow revenue by 17.9% annually to justify the February 12, 2025, closing price. Is this feasible? Its revenue has grown by around 31% over the last ten years, and in a previous article on the company, I explained why I believe the company can achieve at least 20% annual revenue growth over the next ten years. Analysts estimate Nvidia's revenue will grow at a 22.25% CAGR over the next ten years. Hence, a 17.9% yearly growth rate is within the realm of possibility.

However, a nearly 50% FCF margin is likely Nvidia's peak. In a market with rising competition, it is unlikely to sustain a 50% FCF margin over the next ten years. Broadcom (AVGO), a peer, achieved a peak FCF margin of 49.23% over the last ten years but has a median FCF margin of 40.28% in the previous ten years. Let's assume NVIDIA's FCF averages 40% over the next ten years; at an 18% annual growth rate, the estimated intrinsic value would be $106.19. Assuming the company grows revenue at analysts' estimated growth rate of around 22% annually over the next ten years, the estimated intrinsic value would be $143.62. A revenue growth rate of 20.8% over the next ten years at an average FCF margin of 40% would justify the February 12, 2025, stock price of $131.14.

The above reverse DCF uses many assumptions, and no one should believe that the company will hit any of the numbers above exactly. I used the above reverse DCF to loosely determine what the current stock price implies about the market's expectations for the company's FCF margin and revenue growth rates.

Nvidia's price-to-FCF is 57.63, well below its three-, five-, and seven-year median. It only exceeds its ten-year median. Some may consider the stock undervalued because of where its price-to-FCF trades compared to its medians. However, Seeking Alpha's quant grades the stock's valuation as a D-.

Risks

Nvidia had at least three customers, who accounted for more than 10% of revenue in the three- and nine-month period ending on October 27. Additionally, it has an indirect customer who purchased through customer C and resellers, which makes forecasting demand from that customer much more difficult. Nvidia's reliance on a small number of customers means the company has a customer concentration risk. Nvidia could underperform investors' revenue and profitability expectations if any of its top customers have financial issues or cancel or reduce orders.

Additionally, the AI market is rapidly evolving. If Nvidia misses a new industry trend or fails to adapt quickly to industry changes, it could lose market share in a competitive market. Although I don't think DeepSeek's technology presents a real threat to Nvidia's business, it made people aware that if a company or technology does appear that can legitimately reduce the need for increasingly more powerful chips to train and run AI models, it could potentially threaten Nvidia's position as top AI chip provider by making price-performant chips more desirable.

Nvidia stock remains a buy

As I said in a previous article, most buy-and-hold investors would be better off waiting for the stock's FCF yield to rise above 2% before buying. Despite the stock's high returns over the last several years, I have been leery about recommending it for long-term investors since it may not have been a good value to invest in long-term since 2022. At an FCF yield of 1.73%, risk-averse investors should avoid it, considering a potential selloff could be steep if the company misses revenue, EPS, and FCF expectations.

However, this company has a high potential upside for at least the next year or two. Analysts expect revenue to grow 51.69% year over year in FY 2026, which is impressive for a company with $113.27 billion in TTM revenue. That revenue growth comes on the back of Blackwell's ramping over the next year and recent earnings reports from cloud companies indicating they are increasing CapEx in 2025, with much of that spending likely winding up with NVIDIA; aggressive growth investors interested in the continuing adoption of AI and willing to accept the risk of the stock's potential downside should consider buying. I rate Nvidia a buy for aggressive growth investors only.
